repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
No LuaLS types for event-data fields (autocmd ev.data). Event data
  structures are only documented ad hoc in scattered locations (e.g.
  ProgressMessage in vim/ui.lua), making them undiscoverable in editors.

  Solution:
  Add `data` table to `src/nvim/auevents.lua` specifying field names
  and types for all 12 events that provide ev.data: LspAttach,
  LspDetach, LspNotify, LspProgress, LspRequest, LspTokenUpdate,
  MarkSet, PackChanged, PackChangedPre, Progress, TermRequest,
  TermResponse.

  Update `src/gen/gen_eval_files.lua` to generate `@class` types
  (e.g. `vim.event.lspattach.data`, `vim.event.markset.data`) in
  `runtime/lua/vim/_meta/events.lua`.

  Add the generated file to `src/gen/gen_vimdoc.lua` lua config for
  doc generation in lua.txt.

  Closes #38390

comment_data.body: Are these really worth adding here? Why not just maintain the type annotations as source? What does this extra machinery gain?

See https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/38390

We've discussed this before, and my takeaway was that I'm fine with using the luals / `_meta` files as the "source of truth" if we ship a builtin way for users/plugins to programmatically discover those structures.

Otherwise, without that, we aren't making progress towards https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19918

Side-note: it also would be rather confusing for `auevents.lua` to contain some info but then some other meta file contains other info.

I'm still not convinced there's a real need for #19918. Looking through the linked issues, I'm not seeing a specific problem this solves yet.

​If introspection is needed, it'd be better to have a general solution for plugins instead of special-casing internals with one-off logic.

I actually prefer keeping the files separate; keeping them together requires a custom DSL and more machinery, whereas separate files keep the source in a standard format. I'm wary of the redundancy as it creates ambiguity, and that ambiguity leads to confusion regarding the intent.

Like I said, " if we ship a builtin way for users/plugins to programmatically discover those structures" then the debate is moot. 

re introspection in general https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19918 , it's part of the project goals.

> re introspection in general https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19918 , it's part of the project goals.

That's a bit hand wavy.

#19918 is mostly about where introspection should live with most of it completed. There is just a small point in todo: "Provide a util function that makes it easy for plugins to get a Lua structure from docstring annotations.". I'm not sure how that relates to any project goals, especially since the project goals predate docstring annotations. Seems like scope creep.

Tools like https://crates.io/crates/emmylua_doc_cli already exists for parsing annotations. Why does Nvim need to provide something (other than said project goals)?

I don't mean that particular recipe suggested at https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19918 is a goal, but only the general goal of exposing things in a structured way. 

If there is a builtin way to get structure from annotations then that satisfies the goal.
Is there something i should take care of ?

comment_regular_data.body:
> https://github.com/LuaLS/lua-language-server/issues/1861 or something else, I've tried to do so but with no luck yet, do you have some ideas?) or should we provide these classes first?

I think any such move should be in the context of migrating to emmyluals, which has much better generics support. @lewis6991

> The reason why I haven't opened a PR yet is that I'm thinking whether we can achieve automatic type inference without manual annotations (like ---@cast). Currently, our approaches require users to explicitly specify the type to get LSP support for ev.data.

I think it's unlikely and probably not even worth trying to infer typing for `ev.data`. `@cast` is probably the cleanest we can achieve IMO.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: feat(events): define event-data types for autocmd ev.data
